blocks.title: Hidden Routes in First-Passage Time
blocks.spoken_text: In recent months, I have been developing a research line around one question: if a random walker can reach the same target through more than one effective route, what can the first-passage distribution tell us about those hidden routes? The shape I care about is not just one peak, but an early peak, a valley, and then a delayed branch. Today I will start from a realistic one-dimensional ring with a directed shortcut, move to two-dimensional spatial geometries, and then finish with the next step, which is competition between targets as well as competition between routes.
blocks.timing_prompt: Open with the one-question roadmap: 1D ring, 2D geometry, then many-target outlook.

blocks.title: What Is First-Passage Time?
blocks.spoken_text: Let me define the quantity first. A random walk is just a stochastic process that moves step by step. The first-passage time asks when the walker reaches the target for the first time. We repeat that many times and collect those first-hit times into a distribution. This matters because one average value hides too much structure. If the system really has one dominant arrival route, we usually see one dominant timescale. But if two route families survive with different delays, then the full distribution can keep both of them visible.
blocks.timing_prompt: Use the animation to teach first hit from zero background. Point out that the path stops once the target is reached.

blocks.title: Why a Double Peak Matters
blocks.spoken_text: This is why the shape itself matters. A single peak usually means one dominant arrival scale. A double peak suggests that two route families remain statistically visible. The valley between them is also meaningful, because it is the interval where neither family is dominating. So the distribution is not just output. It is compressed mechanism information. That intuition motivates the rest of the talk.
blocks.timing_prompt: Keep this page intuitive and light. The audience only needs to accept that shape can encode hidden route structure.

blocks.title: Single Target on a Ring
blocks.spoken_text: I begin with the simplest realistic single-target model: a periodic ring, one absorbing target, and one directed shortcut. The left schematic shows the actual geometry, with the shortcut as a straight edge from one ring site to another. The right panel is a real comparison from the ring study. One regime stays broad and effectively single-peaked. Another regime keeps a visible early branch and a visible delayed branch. So the lesson is not that every shortcut produces bimodality. The lesson is that bimodality appears only when two route families remain both separated in time and non-negligible in weight.
blocks.timing_prompt: Use the ring to make fast branch versus delayed branch feel concrete. This is the cleanest minimal mechanism page.

blocks.title: Route Design Changes the Distribution
blocks.spoken_text: The next step in the recent work was to ask what controls the delayed branch. The answer is more specific than simply saying that a shortcut exists. As the shortcut destination is retuned, the late branch changes both in strength and in timing. In some destination ranges it becomes clearer; in others it weakens again. So the effect is not monotone. This is the real single-target result I want to stress: route design is part of the physics. The distribution responds to concrete changes in branch timing and branch weight, not just to a vague topological modification.
blocks.timing_prompt: Point to both plots: one changes height, the other changes peak location. Emphasize non-monotone dependence on destination.

blocks.title: Which Spatial Configurations Create a Double Peak?
blocks.spoken_text: Once the ring mechanism is clear, the next question is what kinds of spatial configuration can keep a double peak visible in two dimensions. On the left is the one-target partition picture: corridor, outside region, and target. On the right is a two-target companion drawn in the same grammar. The point is that the 1D story survives in 2D, but geometry now decides whether the two timescales persist. In the one-target case, the competition is between direct corridor transport and delayed outside-return motion. In the two-target case, route competition is still there, but target competition appears on top of it.
blocks.timing_prompt: Sell the continuity: same mechanism language, richer geometry. Use the matching visual grammar to make the jump feel natural.

blocks.title: Outside Excursion or Membrane Crossing?
blocks.spoken_text: This leads to the key one-target mechanism question in 2D. Is the delayed branch mainly an outside-excursion effect, or mainly a membrane-crossing effect? The left panel shows the symmetric corridor geometry. On the right, the upper chart tracks outside-budget separation between the valley and peak two, while the lower chart tracks membrane-related separation. The important message is that peak two separates from the valley more strongly through outside-time budget than through membrane probability alone. So membrane passage matters, but it is not the whole explanation by itself.
blocks.timing_prompt: Slow down here. This is the deepest mechanism page. Give one clear takeaway: outside budget separates valley and peak2 more strongly.

blocks.title: From One Target to Many Potential Targets
blocks.spoken_text: The next natural step is to move beyond one target. With two targets, a peak may already encode not just a route family, but a route-target pair. The representative split on the left shows exactly that kind of target preference across windows. Once there are many possible targets, more mechanisms can appear: nearby targets can screen far ones, target classes can compete, and rare late winners may survive at long times. So the open question is no longer just whether the distribution is bimodal. It is what physical mechanism is making it bimodal once route competition and target competition are both present.
blocks.timing_prompt: End the physics story with momentum. Keep the future direction focused on target competition rather than listing too many side paths.

blocks.title: Minimal Mathematical Backbone
blocks.spoken_text: Let me end with the smallest mathematical backbone behind all of these figures. In two dimensions, the probability mass evolves by a discrete-time master equation on the lattice. The target enters as an absorbing condition through the first-hit time T. From T we define the survival probability S of not yet hitting, and then the first-passage distribution f of first hitting at step t. So although I have shown the story visually, the whole mechanism language is still anchored in a very standard stochastic process: a lattice walk, an absorbing target, and the first-hit distribution generated by that evolution.
blocks.timing_prompt: Use this as a compact mathematical anchor, not as a derivation page. One minute is enough.
